original_abstract: The Good Samaritan in the Gospel of Luke is one of the most recognizable parables in the Christian tradition. While existing scholarship frequently analyzes the narrative through the lens of ethnic tension, this study seeks to re-evaluate the text by prioritizing the impact of religious discord. The historical animosity between Jews and Samaritans was deeply rooted in theological disputes, exemplified by the forced imposition of Judaism upon the Samaritans during the reign of John Hyrcanus. Utilizing a descriptive analytical approach, this research argues that the parable transcends sectarian boundaries. The findings suggest that the narrative serves as a profound call for universal human values, emphasizing social equality, radical compassion, and a totality of service that overrides historical and religious divides.
humanized_abstract: Of all the parables in the Gospel of Luke, none has become more iconic than the Good Samaritan. Much of the literature on the Good Samaritan focuses upon its relationship to ethnic tensions within Luke-Acts, yet this research seeks to bring the religious tensions that formed the basis of those ethnic tensions into sharper focus, in an attempt to illuminate the text of Luke 10.25-37 in a more thorough-going manner. The tensions between Jews and Samaritans were longstanding and multifaceted, arising primarily from religious conflict. Of particular relevance to this discussion is the forced imposition of Judaism upon the Samaritans during the reign of John Hyrcanus. Within this context, a descriptive analytical approach reveals the parable of the Good Samaritan to be capable of being interpreted in a way that transcends its seemingly sectarian parameters. Beyond the particularities of the Samaritan-Jewish conflict, the parable functions within Luke-Acts to summon all people to adhere to universal principles of justice, equality, unusual mercy and an inclusivity of service that transcends ethnicity and religion.
